feynman.0: Light is just a pair of changing electric and magnetic fields, travelling together. On this page we’ll figure out what actually does the wiggling in a light wave, why every electromagnetic wave races along at the very same speed in a vacuum, and what happens when a wave runs into its own reflection and the two pile up into a standing wave.
feynman.1: Back in Unit 3, every field had an owner. A charge dragged its electric field along wherever it went; a current kept the magnetic field wrapped around its wire. But light shows up when a charge accelerates: the field around it has to rearrange itself, and that rearrangement doesn’t stay put — it travels outward as a disturbance in the field itself.
feynman.2: And here’s the lovely part: the disturbance keeps itself going. A changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field makes a changing electric field, so each one keeps building the other right back up. Once you’ve launched the pair, it needs nothing else — no charge, no current, no medium to ride on. It just sails through empty space at the speed of light.
feynman.3: So light is a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by the acceleration of charges. The electric field swings back and forth in one plane, the magnetic field swings in the plane at right angles to it, and both are square-on to the direction the wave is heading, as you can see in Figure 6.1. Compare that with sound, which is a longitudinal jiggling of some medium and simply can’t cross a vacuum. An electromagnetic wave is a wiggling of fields, not of matter — and that’s exactly why sunlight gets all the way to Earth across empty space.
feynman.4: Any charge that accelerates radiates. Drive electrons up and down a broadcast antenna and you get radio waves; let electrons jiggle around inside hot atoms and you get visible light. And don’t forget that going in a circle counts as accelerating too — a charge moving around a circle at constant speed is forever changing its direction, so it radiates the whole time.
feynman.5: Key idea Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by accelerating charges: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field keep regenerating each other, perpendicular to each other and to the direction of travel, with no medium needed at all.
feynman.6: Connects The Australian Synchrotron from 8 · Motors & accelerators is built to be a light source on purpose: electrons forced around the storage ring are accelerating the whole way round, and accelerating charges radiate. Unit 3 AOS 3 hands you the matching idea — changing magnetic fields pushing currents along — when you get to how electricity is generated.
feynman.7: Radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays and gamma rays are all the very same kind of thing: changing electric and magnetic fields regenerating each other. The only way they differ is in frequency and wavelength. In a vacuum, all electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
feynman.8: A wave’s frequency is fixed by whatever made it — by how fast the charges that produced it were oscillating — and it doesn’t change as the wave travels along. And since the product fλ is pinned at c in a vacuum, frequency and wavelength have to trade off against each other: double the frequency and you halve the wavelength.
feynman.9: Lined up from lowest frequency to highest, the electromagnetic spectrum goes radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma. The visible band is just a thin little window in the middle of all that, running from about 750 nm at the red end to about 380 nm at the violet end. The lines drawn between neighbouring bands are really just conventions about how we use them, not changes in the physics: an X-ray and a radio wave obey exactly the same wave equation.
feynman.10: Trap Higher frequency does not mean faster. In a vacuum a gamma ray and a radio wave travel at exactly the same speed, c; the gamma ray’s higher frequency is paid for by a proportionally shorter wavelength, never by going faster.
feynman.11: Kenji shines a green laser pointer across the lab and reads off the label: wavelength 532 nm. He treats the beam as an electromagnetic wave travelling at c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
feynman.12: Formula: c = fλ, so f = cλ with λ = 532 nm = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m.
feynman.14: Answer: f = 5.6 × 10¹⁴ Hz. Every visible wavelength lands you on this scale — hundreds of terahertz, way up above the hundreds of kilohertz of AM radio.
feynman.15: When a travelling wave hits a fixed end, it bounces back and comes home upside down. Now the string is carrying two waves of the same frequency and amplitude running in opposite directions, and at every point and every instant their displacements simply add together — that’s superposition.
feynman.16: At certain spots the two waves always show up with opposite displacements and wipe each other out. Those spots of permanent zero displacement are the nodes. Halfway between the nodes sit the antinodes, where the string swings with twice the amplitude of either travelling wave on its own; neighbouring nodes are λ/2 apart, and so are neighbouring antinodes. The pattern doesn’t go left or right at all: it’s a standing wave, built from a travelling wave superposed with its own reflection.
feynman.17: A string tied down at both ends has to have a node at each end — and the study design only deals with standing waves that have nodes at both ends. Between two fixed ends a distance L apart, a standing wave can only form if a whole number of half-wavelengths fits in exactly: L = nλ/2. Turn that around and the wavelengths it’ll allow are:
feynman.18: Here n is the harmonic number: n = 1 is the fundamental (the first harmonic), the lowest frequency the string can hold; n = 2 is the second harmonic, and so on up. The value of n is just the number of antinodes, and the number of nodes is n + 1, once you count both ends. And because fn = nf1, the frequencies it allows are whole-number multiples of the fundamental, as shown in Figure 6.2.
feynman.19: Key idea A standing wave is a travelling wave superposed with its own inverted reflection. The nodes never budge; with nodes pinned at both ends only the wavelengths λn = 2L/n will fit, so the string can only hold the frequencies fn = nf1.
feynman.20: Ishva stretches a light string 1.20 m long between two rigid clamps and drives it with a little oscillator. Waves run along this string at 240 m s⁻¹, and she slowly cranks the driving frequency up from zero.
feynman.21: Formula: standing waves need λn = 2Ln, so the fundamental (n = 1) has λ1 = 2 × 1.20 = 2.40 m.
feynman.23: Evaluation: f1 = 100 Hz. The third harmonic has λ3 = 2 × 1.20 ÷ 3 = 0.800 m, so f3 = 3f1 = 300 Hz (check: 240 ÷ 0.800 = 300 Hz).
feynman.24: Answer: the string first locks into a standing wave at 100 Hz, with a single antinode between the clamps, and then again at 200 Hz, 300 Hz and every whole-number multiple of 100 Hz. At the third harmonic, 300 Hz, the string shows three antinodes and four nodes — two at the clamped ends and two in between.
feynman.25: Trap A standing-wave diagram is an envelope, not a snapshot frozen in time. The antinodes swing back and forth between the solid and dashed extremes, and twice in every period the whole string sweeps flat through the middle. So if a question asks what the string looks like half a period later, it wants the mirror image of where it started, not the same shape over again.
feynman.26: Connects This condition — only certain wavelengths fit between two fixed points — comes back in 16 · Atoms and spectra, where an electron trapped in an atom behaves like a wave that has to close up on itself, and this same standing-wave picture starts to hint at why atomic energies only come in certain values.

sagan.0: Light is a travelling pair of changing electric and magnetic fields, woven together and rushing through the dark. On this page we ask what it is that oscillates within a wave of light, why every electromagnetic wave races through the vacuum at one and the same speed, and how a wave, meeting its own reflection, conspires with itself to stand perfectly still.
sagan.1: In Unit 3, every field belonged to a source, faithfully attending the thing that made it. A charge carried its electric field wherever it went; a current sustained the magnetic field encircling its wire. But light is born of a more dramatic act — light arises when a charge accelerates. The field surrounding it can no longer keep pace; it must rearrange itself, and that rearrangement does not stay home. It travels outward, a disturbance racing away through the field itself.
sagan.2: And here is the wonder of it: the disturbance sustains itself, needing nothing further. A changing electric field calls a changing magnetic field into being, and a changing magnetic field returns the favour, summoning a changing electric field — so that each forever regenerates the other, hand over hand, all the way across space. Once launched, this travelling pair asks for no charge, no current, no medium of any kind. It propagates through the empty void at the speed of light.
sagan.3: Light, then, is a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by the acceleration of charges. The electric field oscillates in one plane, the magnetic field oscillates in the plane at right angles to it, and both lie perpendicular to the direction in which the wave travels, as shown in Figure 6.1. Sound is a different creature altogether — a longitudinal trembling of some medium, and it cannot cross a vacuum at all. But an electromagnetic wave is an oscillation of fields, not of matter, and that is why the light of the Sun can reach the Earth across the empty reaches of space.
sagan.4: Every accelerating charge radiates — every last one. Electrons driven up and down a broadcasting antenna pour out radio waves; electrons jostling within hot atoms pour out visible light. And do not forget that circular motion is acceleration too — a charge swept around a circle at constant speed is forever changing its direction, and so it radiates without ceasing.
sagan.5: Key idea Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by accelerating charges: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field forever regenerate each other, each perpendicular to the other and to the direction of travel, and no medium is required to carry them.
sagan.6: Connects The Australian Synchrotron from 8 · Motors & accelerators is, by its very design, a source of light: the electrons forced around its storage ring are accelerating continuously, and accelerating charges radiate. Unit 3 AOS 3 furnishes the partner idea — changing magnetic fields driving currents into existence — when at last we come to study how electricity is generated.
sagan.7: Radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays and gamma rays — all of them are the very same kind of wave: changing electric and magnetic fields regenerating each other endlessly. They differ only in frequency and wavelength, in nothing more. In a vacuum, all electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
sagan.8: The frequency of a wave is fixed by its source — by how swiftly the charges that gave it birth oscillated — and it does not change as the wave journeys onward. And because the product fλ is held fast at c in a vacuum, frequency and wavelength are bound together in inverse proportion: double the frequency, and you halve the wavelength.
sagan.9: Ordered from the lowest frequency to the highest, the electromagnetic spectrum runs radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma. The visible band, all that our eyes can perceive of this vast procession, is the narrowest of windows within it — from about 750 nm at the red end to about 380 nm at the violet. The boundaries between one band and its neighbour are conventions of human use, not changes in the physics itself: an X-ray and a radio wave obey exactly the same wave equation.
sagan.10: Trap A higher frequency does not mean a higher speed — let no one persuade you otherwise. In a vacuum a gamma ray and a radio wave travel at exactly the same speed, c; the gamma ray's greater frequency is answered by a proportionally shorter wavelength, never, ever by a greater speed.
sagan.11: Kenji aims a green laser pointer across the laboratory and reads the label upon it: wavelength 532 nm. He pictures the beam as an electromagnetic wave travelling at c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
sagan.12: Formula: c = fλ, so f = cλ with λ = 532 nm = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m.
sagan.14: Answer: f = 5.6 × 10¹⁴ Hz. Every wavelength our eyes can see yields a frequency on this dizzying scale — hundreds of terahertz, soaring far above the mere hundreds of kilohertz of AM radio.
sagan.15: When a travelling wave reaches a fixed end, it cannot go on; it reflects, and returns inverted, flipped upon itself. The string now carries two waves of the same frequency and amplitude, hurrying in opposite directions, and at every point and every instant their displacements add together — this is superposition.
sagan.16: At certain points the two waves always arrive with opposite displacements, and there they cancel utterly. These points of permanent stillness, of perpetual zero displacement, are the nodes. Halfway between the nodes lie the antinodes, where the string swings with twice the amplitude of either travelling wave alone; adjacent nodes stand λ/2 apart, and adjacent antinodes likewise. And the pattern itself travels neither to the left nor to the right — it holds its place: it is a standing wave, born of the superposition of a travelling wave and its own reflection.
sagan.17: A string fixed at both ends must have a node at each end — and the study design concerns itself only with standing waves bearing nodes at both ends. Between two fixed ends a distance L apart, a standing wave can form only when a whole number of half-wavelengths fits exactly between them: L = nλ/2. Rearranged, the wavelengths that nature permits are:
sagan.18: Here n is the harmonic number: n = 1 is the fundamental (the first harmonic), the lowest note the string can sustain; n = 2 is the second harmonic, and so onward without end. The value of n equals the number of antinodes, and the number of nodes is n + 1, counting both ends. Because fn = nf1, the frequencies that are allowed are whole-number multiples of the fundamental — a tidy ladder of tones, as shown in Figure 6.2.
sagan.19: Key idea A standing wave is the superposition of a travelling wave and its inverted reflection. The nodes never stir; with nodes at both ends only the wavelengths λn = 2L/n will fit, so the string can sustain only the frequencies fn = nf1.
sagan.20: Ishva stretches a light string of length 1.20 m between two rigid clamps and drives it with a small oscillator. Waves travel along this string at 240 m s⁻¹, and she raises the driving frequency slowly, patiently, from zero upward.
sagan.21: Formula: standing waves require λn = 2Ln, so the fundamental (n = 1) has λ1 = 2 × 1.20 = 2.40 m.
sagan.23: Evaluation: f1 = 100 Hz. The third harmonic has λ3 = 2 × 1.20 ÷ 3 = 0.800 m, so f3 = 3f1 = 300 Hz (check: 240 ÷ 0.800 = 300 Hz).
sagan.24: Answer: the string first awakens into a standing wave at 100 Hz, with a single antinode swinging between the clamps, and it stirs again at 200 Hz, 300 Hz and every whole-number multiple of 100 Hz thereafter. At the third harmonic, 300 Hz, the string displays three antinodes and four nodes — two at the clamped ends and two more between them.
sagan.25: Trap A standing-wave diagram is an envelope, not a frozen photograph. The antinodes sweep ceaselessly between the solid and the dashed extremes, and twice in every period the whole string sweeps flat through zero. A question that asks for the shape of the string half a period later wants the mirror image of the starting shape — not the same shape returned to you unchanged.
sagan.26: Connects This condition — that only certain wavelengths may fit between two fixed points — returns to us in 16 · Atoms and spectra, where an electron bound within an atom behaves as a wave that must close upon itself, and this very standing-wave picture begins to whisper why the energies of atoms come only in certain discrete values, and never in between.

greene.0: Picture a travelling pair of changing electric and magnetic fields, locked together, sweeping through space — that is light. greene.1: In Unit 3, every field belonged to a source. But light arises from something more dramatic — light arises when a charge accelerates. The field around it must rearrange itself, and that rearrangement does not stay put. It travels outward, a disturbance rippling through the field itself.
greene.2: And here is the elegant part: the disturbance sustains itself. A changing electric field calls a changing magnetic field into being, and that changing magnetic field returns the favour, calling forth a changing electric field — each one forever regenerating the other. Once launched, this travelling pair needs nothing more: no charge, no current, no medium to carry it. It propagates through utterly empty space at the speed of light.

bronowski.0: Light is a travelling pair of changing electric and magnetic fields, moving together through emptiness. bronowski.1: In Unit 3 every field belonged to a source and stayed faithful to it. A charge carried its electric field wherever it went; a current held the magnetic field about its wire. It travels outward, a disturbance in the field itself, set loose from the charge that made it.
bronowski.2: And the disturbance, once made, sustains itself. A changing electric field calls a changing magnetic field into being, and a changing magnetic field calls back a changing electric field, so that each forever regenerates the other. Here is the wonder of it: once launched, the travelling pair needs no charge, no current, no medium to carry it — it makes its own way through empty space at the speed of light.
bronowski.3: Light is therefore a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by the acceleration of charges. The electric field oscillates in one plane, the magnetic field in the plane at right angles to it, and both stand perpendicular to the direction in which the wave travels, as shown in Figure 6.1. Sound is another matter entirely — a longitudinal trembling of some medium, and where there is no medium there can be no sound; it cannot cross a vacuum. bronowski.4: Every accelerating charge radiates — there are no exceptions. levin.0: Light is a travelling pair of changing fields, electric and magnetic, forever leaning on each other. This page asks what oscillates inside a light wave, why every electromagnetic wave keeps the same speed through empty space, and how a wave laid over its own reflection holds still as a standing wave.
levin.1: In Unit 3, every field belonged to something. A charge carried its electric field wherever it went; a current held the magnetic field around its wire. Light begins the moment a charge accelerates: the field around it must rearrange itself, and that rearrangement runs outward — a disturbance not in any substance, but in the field itself.
levin.2: And the disturbance feeds on its own motion. A changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field, which gives rise to a changing electric field, each one forever calling the other back into being. Once it is loosed, the travelling pair needs nothing — no charge, no current, no medium — and crosses empty space at the speed of light.
levin.3: So light is a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by the acceleration of charges. The electric field swings in one plane, the magnetic field in the plane at right angles to it, and both lie perpendicular to the direction of travel, as shown in Figure 6.1. Sound is a different creature — a longitudinal trembling of matter that dies in a vacuum. An electromagnetic wave is an oscillation of fields, not of stuff, and that is why sunlight reaches us across the emptiness.
levin.4: Every accelerating charge radiates. Electrons driven up and down a broadcast antenna pour out radio waves; electrons shaking inside hot atoms pour out visible light. Even circular motion is acceleration — a charge sweeping around a circle at constant speed is changing direction at every instant, and so it radiates without pause.
levin.5: Key idea Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by accelerating charges: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field regenerate each other, perpendicular to each other and to the direction of travel, with no medium required.
levin.6: Connects The Australian Synchrotron from 8 · Motors & accelerators is a light source on purpose: electrons forced around the storage ring accelerate without rest, and accelerating charges radiate. Unit 3 AOS 3 hands you the partner idea — changing magnetic fields driving currents — when you come to electricity generation.
levin.7: Radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays — all one family, all the same thing: changing electric and magnetic fields regenerating each other. They differ only in frequency and wavelength. In a vacuum, all electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
levin.8: A wave’s frequency is set at its birth — by how quickly the charges that made it oscillated — and it does not change as the wave travels on. Since the product fλ is held fast at c in a vacuum, frequency and wavelength move against each other: double the frequency and you halve the wavelength.
levin.9: From lowest frequency to highest, the electromagnetic spectrum runs radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma. The visible band is a narrow window cut into it, reaching from about 750 nm at the red edge to about 380 nm at the violet. The borders between neighbouring bands are conventions of use, not breaks in the physics: an X-ray and a radio wave answer to exactly the same wave equation.
levin.10: Trap Higher frequency does not mean higher speed. In a vacuum a gamma ray and a radio wave travel at exactly the same speed, c; the greater frequency of the gamma ray is paid for by a proportionally shorter wavelength, never by a greater speed.
levin.11: Kenji shines a green laser pointer across the laboratory and reads the fine print on its label: wavelength 532 nm. He treats the beam as an electromagnetic wave travelling at c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
levin.12: Formula: c = fλ, so f = cλ with λ = 532 nm = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m.
levin.14: Answer: f = 5.6 × 10¹⁴ Hz. Every visible wavelength lands on this scale — hundreds of terahertz, far above the hundreds of kilohertz of AM radio.
levin.15: When a travelling wave runs into a fixed end, it turns back inverted. Now the string holds two waves at once — same frequency, same amplitude, travelling against each other — and at every point and every instant their displacements simply add. This is superposition.
levin.16: At certain points the two waves always arrive with opposite displacements and cancel. These points of permanent stillness are nodes. Halfway between them lie the antinodes, where the string swings with twice the amplitude of either travelling wave alone; adjacent nodes sit λ/2 apart, and adjacent antinodes likewise. The pattern goes nowhere, neither left nor right: it is a standing wave, born from the superposition of a travelling wave and its reflection.
levin.17: A string clamped at both ends must hold a node at each end — the study design treats only standing waves with nodes at both ends. Between two fixed ends a distance L apart, a standing wave can take shape only when a whole number of half-wavelengths fits exactly: L = nλ/2. Rearranged, the wavelengths it will allow are:
levin.18: Here n is the harmonic number: n = 1 is the fundamental, the first harmonic, the lowest note the string can hold; n = 2 is the second harmonic, and so on upward. The value of n equals the number of antinodes, and the number of nodes is n + 1, counting both ends. Because fn = nf1, the allowed frequencies are whole-number multiples of the fundamental, as shown in Figure 6.2.
levin.19: Key idea A standing wave is the superposition of a travelling wave and its inverted reflection. Nodes never move; with nodes at both ends only the wavelengths λn = 2L/n fit, so the string sustains only the frequencies fn = nf1.

The antinodes swing between the solid and dashed extremes, and twice in every period the whole string sweeps through the flat position. A question asking for the shape of the string half a period later expects the mirror image of the starting shape, not the same shape.

czerski.0: Light is a travelling pair of changing electric and magnetic fields, forever propping each other up as they race across the room. veritasium.0: You probably picture light as a thing — a beam, a ray, something that travels. But there's nothing solid there at all. Light is a travelling pair of changing electric and magnetic fields, and that's the whole story. This page works out what's actually oscillating in a light wave, why every electromagnetic wave moves at exactly the same speed in a vacuum no matter what it is, and how a wave laid on top of its own reflection builds a standing wave.
veritasium.1: All through Unit 3, every field belonged to a source and stayed with it. A charge dragged its electric field along wherever it went; a current held up the magnetic field looped around its wire. So you might expect a field always needs something to anchor it. But watch what happens when a charge accelerates: the field around it can't keep up, it has to rearrange — and that rearrangement doesn't stay put. It travels outward, a disturbance in the field itself.
veritasium.2: Here's the surprising part: that disturbance keeps itself alive. A changing electric field generates a changing magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field generates a changing electric field, so each one is forever rebuilding the other. You'd think it would need wires, or charges, or at least some stuff to travel through. It needs none of them. Once it's launched, the travelling pair requires no charge, no current and no medium — it propagates through completely empty space at the speed of light.
veritasium.3: So light is a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by the acceleration of charges. The electric field oscillates in one plane, the magnetic field oscillates in the perpendicular plane, and both of them point sideways to the direction of travel, as shown in Figure 6.1. Now compare that with sound. You might lump them together as "waves," but sound is a longitudinal vibration of a medium and simply cannot cross a vacuum — it has nothing to shake. An electromagnetic wave is an oscillation of fields, not of matter, and that's the reason sunlight crosses millions of kilometres of empty space to reach you.
veritasium.4: And it's not just antennas and lasers — any accelerating charge radiates. Electrons driven up and down a broadcast antenna throw off radio waves; electrons jiggling inside hot atoms throw off visible light. Here's the part people forget: moving in a circle counts as accelerating. A charge going around a circle at constant speed is changing direction every instant, so it never stops accelerating — and it radiates the whole way round.
veritasium.5: Key idea Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by accelerating charges: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field keep regenerating each other, perpendicular to each other and to the direction of travel, with no medium required.
veritasium.6: Connects The Australian Synchrotron from 8 · Motors & accelerators isn't just a particle machine that happens to glow — it's built to be a light source. Electrons forced around the storage ring are accelerating every moment, and accelerating charges radiate. Unit 3 AOS 3 supplies the other half of the picture — changing magnetic fields driving currents — when electricity generation is studied.
veritasium.7: You probably file radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays and gamma rays as seven different things. They're not. They're all the same wave: changing electric and magnetic fields regenerating each other. The only thing that separates them is frequency and wavelength. In a vacuum, all electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ — the gamma ray and the radio wave keep perfect pace.
veritasium.8: It's tempting to think a wave can change its frequency as it travels, but it can't. The frequency is locked in at birth — set by how fast the charges that made it were oscillating — and it stays that way the whole journey. And because the product fλ is pinned to c in a vacuum, frequency and wavelength are inversely proportional: double the frequency and you've halved the wavelength, automatically.
veritasium.9: Run from lowest frequency to highest and the electromagnetic spectrum goes radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma. The light you can actually see is a tiny slit in that range, from about 750 nm at the red end to about 380 nm at the violet end. And here's the thing about the boundaries between the bands — they're not where physics changes, they're just where we decided to draw lines based on how we use them. An X-ray and a radio wave obey exactly the same wave equation.
veritasium.10: Trap It feels like higher frequency should mean more speed — faster wiggling, faster wave. It doesn't. In a vacuum a gamma ray and a radio wave travel at exactly the same speed, c. The gamma ray's extra frequency is paid for by a proportionally shorter wavelength, never by extra speed.

Halfway between them sit the antinodes, where the string swings with twice the amplitude of either travelling wave on its own; adjacent nodes are λ/2 apart, and adjacent antinodes likewise. attenborough.26: Connects This condition — that only certain wavelengths may fit between two fixed points — returns to us in 16 · Atoms and spectra, where an electron bound within an atom behaves as a wave that must close upon itself, and the standing-wave picture begins, quietly, to suggest why the energies of atoms come only in certain values, and no others.

greekepic.0: Light is a travelling pair of changing electric and magnetic fields, journeying together as bound companions. This song proclaims what trembles within a wave of light, why every wave of the electromagnetic kind races at one selfsame speed through the void, and how a wave, met by the shade of its own reflection, raises up a standing wave.
greekepic.1: In Unit 3, every field was the possession of a source, bound to its master. A charge bore its electric field wherever it went; a current sustained the magnetic field encircling its wire. But light is born when a charge accelerates: the field about it must be remade, and that remaking journeys outward as a disturbance in the very body of the field.
greekepic.2: This disturbance feeds upon itself, and needs no other nourishment. A changing electric field begets a changing magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field begets a changing electric field in turn, so each forever fathers the other anew. Once set loose, the travelling pair calls for no charge, no current, no medium at all — it journeys through the empty void at the speed of light.
greekepic.3: Light is therefore a transverse electromagnetic wave produced by the acceleration of charges. The electric field sways within one plane, the magnetic field sways within the plane that crosses it at right angles, and both stand square to the path of travel, as is shown in Figure 6.1. Sound, by contrast, is a longwise trembling of some medium, and can never cross the void; but an electromagnetic wave is a swaying of fields, not of matter, and this is why the light of the Sun reaches the Earth across the empty deep.
greekepic.4: Every accelerating charge sends forth its radiance. Electrons driven up and down a broadcast antenna raise radio waves; electrons swaying within the hearts of hot atoms raise visible light. Circular motion, too, is acceleration — a charge wheeling in a circle at constant speed is forever turning aside from its course, and so it radiates without ceasing.
greekepic.5: Key idea Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave brought forth by accelerating charges: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field father each other anew, the one square to the other and both square to the path of travel, and no medium is required.
greekepic.6: Connects The Australian Synchrotron of 8 · Motors & accelerators is a wellspring of light by its very design: electrons compelled around the storage ring are forever accelerating, and accelerating charges send forth their radiance. Unit 3 AOS 3 furnishes the kindred truth — changing magnetic fields driving currents — when the making of electricity is studied.
greekepic.7: Radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays and gamma rays are all one kindred of wave: changing electric and magnetic fields fathering each other anew. They differ in frequency and wavelength alone. Within the void, all electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
greekepic.8: The frequency of a wave is decreed by its source — by how swiftly the charges that begot it swayed — and it does not waver as the wave journeys on. Because the product fλ is held fast at c within the void, frequency and wavelength are bound in inverse measure: to double the frequency is to halve the wavelength.
greekepic.9: Ordered from the lowest frequency to the highest, the electromagnetic spectrum runs radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma. The visible band is but a narrow window set within it, reaching from about 750 nm at the red end to about 380 nm at the violet end. The borders between neighbouring bands are conventions of use, not turnings of physics: an X-ray and a radio wave keep faith with one selfsame wave equation.
greekepic.10: Trap Mark well: higher frequency does not mean higher speed. Within the void a gamma ray and a radio wave travel at exactly the same speed, c; the greater frequency of the gamma ray is answered by a wavelength shortened in like measure, never by a swifter pace.
greekepic.11: Kenji aims a green laser pointer across the laboratory and reads the word upon its label: wavelength 532 nm. He fashions the beam in his mind as an electromagnetic wave travelling at c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
greekepic.12: Formula: c = fλ, so f = cλ with λ = 532 nm = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m.
greekepic.14: Answer: f = 5.6 × 10¹⁴ Hz. Every visible wavelength yields a frequency upon this lofty scale — hundreds of terahertz, soaring far above the hundreds of kilohertz of AM radio.
greekepic.15: When a travelling wave comes upon a fixed end, it is turned back and returns overturned. The string then bears two waves of one frequency and one amplitude journeying against each other, and their displacements join at every point and in every instant — superposition.
greekepic.16: At certain places the two waves arrive forever with opposing displacements, and so undo one another. These places of unending stillness are nodes. Halfway between the nodes lie the antinodes, where the string sways with twice the amplitude of either travelling wave; neighbouring nodes stand λ/2 apart, and neighbouring antinodes likewise. The pattern journeys neither to the left nor to the right: it is a standing wave, brought forth from the superposition of a travelling wave and its reflection.
greekepic.17: A string made fast at both ends must bear a node at either end — the study design treats only standing waves with nodes at both ends. Between two fixed ends a distance L apart, a standing wave may arise only if a whole number of half-wavelengths is seated there exactly: L = nλ/2. Set in order, the wavelengths that are permitted are:
greekepic.18: Here n is the harmonic number: n = 1 is the fundamental (the first harmonic), the lowest frequency the string can sustain; n = 2 is the second harmonic, and so onward. The value of n equals the number of antinodes, and the number of nodes is n + 1, counting both ends. Because fn = nf1, the frequencies permitted are whole-number multiples of the fundamental, as is shown in Figure 6.2.
greekepic.19: Key idea A standing wave is the superposition of a travelling wave and its overturned reflection. The nodes never stir; with nodes at both ends only the wavelengths λn = 2L/n are seated there, so the string sustains the frequencies fn = nf1 and no others.
greekepic.20: Ishva stretches a light string of length 1.20 m between two unyielding clamps and drives it with a small oscillator. Waves journey along this string at 240 m s⁻¹, and she raises the driving frequency slowly upward from naught.
greekepic.21: Formula: standing waves require λn = 2Ln, so the fundamental (n = 1) has λ1 = 2 × 1.20 = 2.40 m.
greekepic.23: Evaluation: f1 = 100 Hz. The third harmonic has λ3 = 2 × 1.20 ÷ 3 = 0.800 m, so f3 = 3f1 = 300 Hz (check: 240 ÷ 0.800 = 300 Hz).
greekepic.24: Answer: the string first raises a standing wave at 100 Hz, with a single antinode between the clamps, and again at 200 Hz, 300 Hz and at every whole-number multiple of 100 Hz. At the third harmonic, 300 Hz, the string shows three antinodes and four nodes — two at the clamped ends and two between them.
greekepic.25: Trap Mark well: a standing-wave diagram is an envelope, not a shape frozen for all time. The antinodes sway between the solid and the dashed extremes, and twice in every period the whole string passes through the flat repose. A question that asks for the shape of the string half a period onward expects the mirror image of the starting shape, never the same shape.
greekepic.26: Connects This condition — that only certain wavelengths may be seated between two fixed points — returns again in 16 · Atoms and spectra, where an electron bound within an atom bears itself as a wave that must close upon its own beginning, and the standing-wave picture whispers why the energies of atoms come only in certain values.

cnpop.0: 光，说白了就是一对手拉手、不停变脸的电场和磁场，一起在路上狂奔。这一页我们就把三件事掰开揉碎讲清楚：光波里到底是啥在振动？为啥所有电磁波在真空里都跑出一模一样的速度？还有，一个波跟自己的反射叠在一起，怎么就硬生生憋出一个驻波来。
cnpop.1: 在第三单元里，每个场都得认个主——电荷走到哪就把自己的电场背到哪，电流则在导线周围稳稳撑起磁场。可光不一样：光是在电荷加速的那一刻诞生的。电荷一加速，它周围的场就得手忙脚乱地重新排队，而这场重新排队的骚动，会像涟漪一样一路向外扩散，本质上就是场自己身上的一阵抖动。
cnpop.2: 这阵抖动最秀的地方在于：它能自给自足，根本不用外援。变化的电场会现场制造一个变化的磁场，变化的磁场又反手生出一个变化的电场，俩兄弟你生我、我生你，循环不断。一旦发车上路，这对搭档就再也不需要电荷、不需要电流、更不需要任何介质——它能在空无一物的太空里以光速一路狂飙。
cnpop.3: 所以光的真实身份是：一种由电荷加速产生的横向电磁波。电场在一个平面里上下振动，磁场在与之垂直的另一个平面里振动，而且这俩还都跟前进方向互相垂直，活脱脱就是图 6.1 那个样子。反观声音，它是介质的纵向振动，过不了真空这一关；电磁波振的是场，不是物质，这也正是为啥阳光能横跨一整片虚空跑到地球上。
cnpop.4: 划重点：只要电荷加速，它就辐射，没有例外。电子在广播天线里被赶着上蹿下跳，就生出无线电波；电子在炽热原子里疯狂振荡，就生出可见光。别忘了，圆周运动也是加速——一个电荷以恒定速率绕圈跑，方向时时刻刻都在变，所以它也时时刻刻在辐射。
cnpop.5: Key idea 光是由加速电荷产生的横向电磁波：一个变化的电场和一个变化的磁场互相生成、彼此再造，两者相互垂直、又都跟前进方向垂直，全程不需要任何介质。
cnpop.6: Connects 来自 8 · Motors & accelerators 的澳大利亚同步加速器，天生就是个光源：电子被逼着在储存环里绕圈，等于一直在加速，而加速的电荷就会辐射。第三单元 AOS 3 还会补上配套的另一半剧情——变化的磁场驱动电流——等学到发电的时候你就懂了。
cnpop.7: 无线电波、微波、红外线、可见光、紫外线、X 射线、伽马射线——别看名字一个比一个唬人，它们其实是同一种货色：变化的电场和磁场互相再造。它们之间唯一的区别就是频率和波长不一样而已。在真空里，所有电磁波都跑同一个速度，c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹。
cnpop.8: 一个波的频率是它老家定的——取决于当初造出它的那些电荷振得有多快——而且这个频率在波旅行的一路上不会变。由于在真空里乘积 fλ 死死锁定在 c，频率和波长就成了反比的一对冤家：频率翻一倍，波长立马砍一半。
cnpop.9: 从频率最低到最高排个队，整条电磁波谱依次是：无线电波、微波、红外线、可见光、紫外线、X 射线、伽马射线。可见光这一段只是其中一扇窄窄的小窗，红端大约从 750 nm 起步，一路到紫端大约 380 nm 收尾。相邻波段之间的那条分界线，其实是人为按用途划的，并不是物理本身变了天：一条 X 射线和一条无线电波，遵守的是一模一样的波动方程。
cnpop.10: Trap 别踩坑：频率高不等于速度快。在真空里，伽马射线和无线电波跑的速度一模一样，都是 c；伽马射线频率高，是靠按比例缩短的波长来配平的，绝不是靠跑得更快。
cnpop.11: Kenji 拿一支绿色激光笔横着射穿整间实验室，读了一眼标签：波长 532 nm。他把这束光当成一个以 c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ 行进的电磁波来建模。
cnpop.12: 公式：c = fλ，所以 f = cλ，其中 λ = 532 nm = 5.32 × 10⁻⁷ m。
cnpop.14: 答案：f = 5.6 × 10¹⁴ Hz。每一个可见光波长算出来的频率都在这个量级——好几百太赫兹，把 AM 广播那几百千赫兹甩出十万八千里。
cnpop.15: 当一个行进的波撞上一个固定端，它会反弹回来，而且整个倒了个个儿。这下弦上就同时跑着两个波：频率一样、振幅一样，方向却正好相反。它们的位移在每一个点、每一个瞬间都加在一起——这就是叠加。
cnpop.16: 在某些点上，这两个波永远带着相反的位移到场，当场抵消，谁也别想动。这些位移永远为零的点叫波节。两个波节正中间的位置则是波腹，那里弦的振幅能干到任一行进波的两倍；相邻波节之间相距 λ/2，相邻波腹同理。整个图案既不往左也不往右跑：它就是一个驻波，由一个行进波和它的反射波叠加而成。
cnpop.17: 两端固定的弦，每个端点都必须是个波节——考纲只考两端都为波节的驻波。在相距 L 的两个固定端之间，驻波只有在能塞进整数个半波长时才形成得了：L = nλ/2。把它倒腾一下，允许的波长就是：
cnpop.18: 这里的 n 是谐波数：n = 1 是基频（也就是第一谐波），是这根弦能撑住的最低频率；n = 2 是第二谐波，以此类推。n 的数值正好等于波腹的个数，而波节的个数是 n + 1，两端都算上。因为 fn = nf1，所以允许的频率都是基频的整数倍，正如图 6.2 所示。
cnpop.19: Key idea 驻波就是一个行进波和它倒转过来的反射波叠加的产物。波节永远纹丝不动；当两端都是波节时，只有波长 λn = 2L/n 才塞得下，所以这根弦只撑得住频率 fn = nf1。
cnpop.20: Ishva 在两个刚性夹具之间绷起一根长 1.20 m 的轻弦，用一个小振子去带它。波在这根弦上以 240 m s⁻¹ 行进，她把驱动频率从零开始慢慢往上推。
cnpop.21: 公式：驻波要求 λn = 2Ln，所以基频（n = 1）对应 λ1 = 2 × 1.20 = 2.40 m。
cnpop.23: 求值：f1 = 100 Hz。第三谐波的 λ3 = 2 × 1.20 ÷ 3 = 0.800 m，所以 f3 = 3f1 = 300 Hz（验算一下：240 ÷ 0.800 = 300 Hz）。
cnpop.24: 答案：这根弦第一次形成驻波是在 100 Hz，夹具之间就一个波腹；接着在 200 Hz、300 Hz 以及 100 Hz 的每一个整数倍处又会形成。到第三谐波 300 Hz 时，弦上就秀出三个波腹和四个波节——两端夹住的两个，中间还有两个。
cnpop.25: Trap 别被骗：驻波图画的是一个包络线，不是某个冻住的瞬间形状。波腹会在实线和虚线两个极端之间来回振荡，而且每个周期里整根弦有两次会从平直位置穿过。要是题目问你半个周期之后弦是什么形状，它想要的是起始形状的镜像，可不是原封不动的同一个形状。
cnpop.26: Connects 这个条件——两个固定点之间只有某些特定波长塞得进去——会在 16 · Atoms and spectra 里王者归来。那时被束缚在原子里的电子，行为就像一个必须首尾闭合的波，而这幅驻波图景正好暗示了：为啥原子的能量只能取某些特定的值。
